skill.name: routeros-qemu-chr
skill.description: MikroTik RouterOS CHR (Cloud Hosted Router) with QEMU. Use when: running RouterOS in QEMU, booting CHR images, debugging CHR boot failures, setting up VirtIO devices for RouterOS, choosing between SeaBIOS and UEFI boot, configuring QEMU port forwarding for RouterOS REST API, or selecting QEMU acceleration (KVM/HVF/TCG).
skill.summary: Run MikroTik RouterOS CHR virtual machines in QEMU with acceleration support
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Documentation and reference skill for running RouterOS CHR in QEMU. Static analysis flagged 343 patterns, but evaluation reveals these are false positives: shell backtick notation in markdown code examples (not execution), sudo in GitHub Actions CI (expected), MD5 references in kernel history docs (not actual usage), and legitimate acceleration detection commands. All network access targets MikroTik infrastructure for downloading CHR images. Risk level set to LOW due to external command patterns in documentation examples, but no actual malicious code present.

high_findings.title: Documentation Shell Examples Misidentified as Execution
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 264 instances of Ruby/shell backtick notation. These are markdown code blocks showing shell command syntax, not actual command execution. Files are documentation with command examples.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: All backtick patterns appear in markdown code blocks (```sh, ```yaml, ```routeros). The files are documentation with command examples, not executable code.

high_findings.title: sudo Commands in GitHub Actions CI (Expected Behavior)
high_findings.description: GitHub Actions workflow uses sudo for package installation (apt-get install). This is standard CI/CD practice, not privilege escalation risk.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: GitHub Actions runners provide sudo access for package management. Lines 34-48 show apt-get install commands, which is expected behavior.

high_findings.title: nohup for Background QEMU Process (Legitimate Use)
high_findings.description: nohup is used to run QEMU in background during CI testing. This is standard practice for running VMs in CI environments.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Background process spawning is required for CI pipelines to run CHR and execute REST API tests. Known-issues.md documents correct PID handling.

high_findings.title: Base64 HTTP Basic Auth (Standard Practice)
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged btoa('admin:') as weak crypto. This is standard HTTP Basic Auth encoding, not cryptographic weakness.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: btoa() encodes credentials for HTTP Basic Auth to RouterOS REST API. This is the documented authentication method, not MD5 hashing.

medium_findings.title: Network Access to External URLs
medium_findings.description: Skill downloads CHR images from MikroTik infrastructure. URLs point to download.mikrotik.com and cdn.mikrotik.com for official RouterOS images.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All network access targets official MikroTik infrastructure for legitimate RouterOS image downloads. No suspicious external endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Device File Access for Virtualization
medium_findings.description: /dev/kvm access for KVM acceleration detection. This is standard practice for virtualization tooling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: KVM device access is required for hardware acceleration. QEMU documentation skill legitimately requires this access.

medium_findings.title: Temp Directory Access
medium_findings.description: /tmp used for QEMU vars files, serial sockets, and log files. Standard temp file usage for VM management.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Temp directory access is standard for QEMU VM management. Deterministic paths used for vars files, no sensitive data stored.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded IP Addresses (Localhost)
low_findings.description: 127.0.0.1 used for RouterOS REST API and port forwarding. Standard localhost addressing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All IPs are 127.0.0.1 loopback for local CHR access. No external IP addresses for suspicious destinations.

low_findings.title: System Information Commands (Acceleration Detection)
low_findings.description: uname, sysctl, and stat commands used for platform detection. Standard virtualization tooling practice.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Commands detect host OS/architecture to select appropriate QEMU acceleration (KVM/HVF/TCG). Legitimate infrastructure detection.

content.user_title: Run MikroTik RouterOS CHR in QEMU
content.value_statement: MikroTik RouterOS CHR provides a full-featured virtual router for testing and development, but setup requires navigating QEMU options, VirtIO drivers, and firmware configurations. This skill provides complete guidance for running CHR with acceleration, proper VirtIO setup, and REST API integration.
actual_capabilities: Download and boot MikroTik RouterOS CHR images for x86_64 and aarch64 architectures
Configure QEMU with proper VirtIO devices for disk and network performance
Select hardware acceleration (KVM on Linux, HVF on macOS, TCG software fallback)
Set up port forwarding for RouterOS REST API, SSH, WinBox, and API protocol access
Debug boot failures including VirtIO MMIO trap on aarch64 and UEFI pflash setup
Run RouterOS CHR in GitHub Actions CI for automated API testing and schema generation
limitations: QEMU Guest Agent (QGA) requires KVM and does not work under HVF or TCG acceleration
The check-installation command fails on aarch64 due to firmware DTB limitations (skip this check)
Cross-architecture TCG emulation (x86_64 guest on aarch64 host) is not viable due to I/O port emulation issues

use_cases.title: Automated RouterOS REST API Testing in CI
use_cases.description: Run RouterOS CHR as a CI test fixture to validate REST API calls, generate RAML schemas, and test /app YAML configurations without manual router setup.

use_cases.title: Development and Learning Environment
use_cases.description: Boot a free-license CHR instance to explore RouterOS features, test firewall rules, experiment with bridging, and learn networking concepts without production hardware.

use_cases.title: Multi-Architecture Testing
use_cases.description: Test RouterOS configurations across both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures using QEMU with appropriate firmware (SeaBIOS for x86, UEFI for ARM) and acceleration options.

prompt_templates.title: Basic CHR Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up RouterOS CHR in QEMU. I need to download the latest stable image and boot it with KVM acceleration and port 9180 for the REST API.

prompt_templates.title: GitHub Actions CI Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a GitHub Actions workflow that downloads RouterOS CHR, boots it with KVM if available (falls back to TCG), waits for boot, then runs REST API tests.

prompt_templates.title: ARM64 UEFI Boot Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure QEMU to boot RouterOS CHR aarch64 on macOS Apple Silicon. Include the UEFI pflash setup and explicit virtio-blk-pci device configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Debugging Boot Failures
prompt_templates.prompt: RouterOS CHR fails to boot with a blank screen. The disk image uses if=virtio on aarch64. What might be wrong and how do I fix it?

output_examples.input: Boot CHR with KVM acceleration and REST API on port 9180
output_examples.output: QEMU launches with hardware acceleration. RouterOS boots in ~5 seconds. HTTP 200 response from http://127.0.0.1:9180/ confirms readiness. REST API accessible at http://127.0.0.1:9180/rest/ with admin credentials.

output_examples.input: Enable KVM on GitHub Actions Ubuntu runner
output_examples.output: udev rule created at /etc/udev/rules.d/99-kvm4all.rules. KVM device permissions updated to 0666. QEMU successfully opens /dev/kvm for hardware acceleration. Boot time reduced from ~30s to ~5s.

output_examples.input: Configure port forwarding for RouterOS services
output_examples.output: Host ports mapped: 9180→80 (REST API), 9122→22 (SSH), 9728→8728 (API), 9729→8729 (API-SSL), 9291→8291 (WinBox). Services accessible from host via localhost ports.

best_practices: Use explicit -device virtio-blk-pci instead of if=virtio shorthand on aarch64 to avoid the MMIO trap that causes silent boot failures
Check /dev/kvm writability (not just existence) before enabling KVM, and always fall back to TCG gracefully in case KVM is unavailable
Use port forwarding patterns like tcp::9180-:80 instead of hardcoding localhost IPs to make the configuration portable and reusable
anti_patterns: Do not use if=virtio on aarch64 architecture - this resolves to MMIO which RouterOS does not support, causing silent boot failures
Do not skip the KVM permission check - /dev/kvm may exist but be unreadable, and QEMU does not silently fall back to TCG on permission errors
Do not use git pull && git push in concurrent CI builds - use the retry-with-rebase pattern to avoid push rejections from race conditions

faq.question: What is the speed limit on the free CHR license?
faq.answer: The free CHR license limits interface throughput to 1 Mbps. REST API calls, SSH, WinBox, and WebFig access are unaffected. This limit applies only to actual data forwarding between interfaces.

faq.question: Why does QEMU Guest Agent not work with HVF acceleration?
faq.answer: RouterOS QGA daemon only starts when it detects a KVM hypervisor via CPUID. Under HVF (macOS) or TCG (software emulation), CPUID 0x40000000 returns no KVM vendor string, so the daemon never starts. Use Linux with KVM for QGA testing.

faq.question: How do I choose between SeaBIOS and UEFI for CHR boot?
faq.answer: Use SeaBIOS for x86_64 (default, fastest boot). Use UEFI for aarch64 ARM architecture. On x86_64, only SeaBIOS can boot the proprietary boot partition; OVMF cannot read it.

faq.question: Why is my aarch64 CHR stuck at boot with if=virtio?
faq.answer: On aarch64 virt machine, if=virtio resolves to MMIO transport. RouterOS has virtio_pci but not virtio_mmio driver, so the kernel stalls silently. Always use explicit -device virtio-blk-pci on aarch64.

faq.question: Can I run multiple CHR instances simultaneously?
faq.answer: Yes. Use unique host ports for each instance (9180, 9181, 9182 for REST API, etc.). Each instance needs its own disk image and PID tracking for cleanup.

faq.question: What acceleration method should I use?
faq.answer: Use KVM on Linux when host/guest architectures match. Use HVF on macOS Apple Silicon for aarch64 guests. Use TCG as fallback on all platforms when hardware acceleration is unavailable.
